attributes.stem:
A 65-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a history of sharp right upper quadrant pain that began suddenly this morning, lasted for about 20 minutes, and then subsided. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. He works as an accountant and has no relevant travel history.

On examination, he appears well. His pulse rate is 90/min, blood pressure 140/100 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. Abdominal examination is unremarkable with no tenderness or palpable masses. A CT scan of the abdomen is arranged, and a representative slice is shown (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Reassurance and review in three months
attributes.explanation:
The CT image shows a large abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA), which appears to be greater than 5.5 cm in diameter. This is an important incidental finding that requires urgent attention, even though it is likely unrelated to the patient's presenting symptoms of right upper quadrant pain (which may represent biliary colic).

Vascular surgical referral is the most appropriate next step because the size of this aneurysm places the patient at significant risk of rupture. The threshold for surgical intervention is typically 5.5 cm in diameter, as at this size the risk of rupture (with its high mortality rate) begins to outweigh the risks associated with elective surgical repair. The decision between open surgical repair versus endovascular aneurysm repair (EVAR) would be made by the vascular surgeon based on anatomical considerations and patient factors.

Reassurance and review in three months would be inappropriate management for a large AAA. Surveillance without intervention might be suitable for smaller aneurysms (typically <5.5 cm), but not for one of this size where the risk of rupture is significant.

Referral for cholecystectomy might be considered for the patient's right upper quadrant pain if gallstones were identified and thought to be symptomatic. However, even if gallstones were present, the priority would be addressing the more immediately life-threatening AAA before considering cholecystectomy.

Hydatid serology would be appropriate if there were cystic lesions suspicious for hydatid disease. However, the image clearly shows an abdominal aortic aneurysm rather than any cystic structures that might represent hydatid cysts.

CT-guided aspiration has no role in the management of an AAA. This procedure might be considered for certain types of fluid collections or abscesses, but would be contraindicated and potentially catastrophic if attempted on an aneurysm.

The management of AAA is primarily determined by size, with other factors such as growth rate, symptoms, and patient fitness for surgery also being considered. While the patient's presenting complaint appears unrelated to the aneurysm, this incidental finding requires prompt attention and referral to prevent the potentially fatal complication of aneurysm rupture.
learningPoints: Abdominal aortic aneurysms greater than 5.5 cm in diameter generally require surgical intervention due to increased rupture risk.
Incidental findings on imaging may be more clinically significant than the presenting complaint.
Vascular surgical referral is indicated for large abdominal aortic aneurysms even when asymptomatic.
references: Kent KC. Abdominal Aortic Aneurysms. N Engl J Med 2014;371:2101-08. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25427112/

ESVS 2024 Clinical Practice Guidelines on the Management of Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm. In: European Society for Vascular Surgery (ESVS) [online]. Accessed 2025 Jun 25. Available at: https://esvs.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/ESVS-2024-AAA-Guidelines.pdf
